payload.text:
Perfect. Below is a **single, clean, professional MASTER PROMPT** you can paste directly into **Comet Browser AI / Cursor / any strong coding AI** to rebuild **Mati Foundation from scratch** as a **new, premium project**.

This prompt is structured to:

* Avoid architectural mistakes
* Enforce consistency
* Produce production-grade code
* Match NGO / donor expectations
* Be CMS-first, admin-safe, and scalable

---

# ✅ MASTER BUILD PROMPT

**Project: Mati Foundation – Complete NGO Website & CMS**

---

## ROLE & EXPECTATION

You are a **senior full-stack engineer + UX architect** building a **production-ready non-profit website** for an orphanage called **Mati Foundation**, based in **Kenya**.

This is a **new rebuild**, not a patch or update.

Your output must be:

* Clean
* Modular
* Type-safe
* Secure
* Easily editable by non-technical admins
* Visually premium and emotionally trustworthy (donor-ready)

No shortcuts. No experimental hacks.

---

## 1. PROJECT OVERVIEW

Build a **complete, modern, interactive NGO website** for **Mati Foundation**, a non-profit orphanage focused on:

* Child welfare
* Education support
* Community empowerment
* Sponsorship programs
* Transparency and donor trust

The system must include:

1. **Public Website**
2. **AI-powered Chat Assistant**
3. **Secure Admin CMS**
4. **Cloud-based media management**

---

## 2. TECH STACK (MANDATORY)

### Frontend

* **React + TypeScript**
* Client-side rendering
* Hash-based routing (no backend server)

### Styling

* **TailwindCSS (CDN)**
* Utility-first, consistent spacing and colors

### Animations

* **Framer Motion**
* Page transitions
* Micro-interactions
* Scroll-triggered animations

### Backend / Data

* **Firebase Firestore**

  * All website content stored in a single structured document
  * Real-time sync
  * Offline fallback using constants

### Authentication

* **Firebase Authentication**
* Email & password only
* Admin-only access

### Media

* **Cloudinary**
* Direct client-side uploads
* Images & videos
* Auto-optimization enabled

### AI Assistant

* **Google Gemini API**
* Chatbot answers questions about:

  * Mission
  * Programs
  * Sponsorship
  * How to help
* Uses a predefined system prompt based on foundation content

### Module System (IMPORTANT)

* **Browser-native ES Modules**
* Use **importmap in index.html**
* NO bundlers (no Vite, no Webpack)
* All local imports MUST include extensions (`.ts`, `.tsx`)

---

## 3. VISUAL DESIGN SYSTEM

### Typography

* **Headings (Serif):** Playfair Display
* **Body (Sans):** Space Grotesk

### Color Palette

* Primary (Teal): `#0d9488`
* Accent (Dark Teal): `#134e4a`
* Secondary (Light Teal): `#f0fdfa`
* Main Text: `#1e293b`
* Muted Text: `#475569`

### Layout Rules

* Max width: `max-w-7xl`
* Generous white space
* Mobile-first responsive design

### Header

* Transparent initially
* Turns into **glassmorphic sticky navbar** on scroll
* Logo + navigation + highlighted **Donate** button

### Footer

* Navigation links
* Contact info
* Social media icons
* Newsletter form
* Admin panel link

---

## 4. REQUIRED FILE STRUCTURE

```
├── index.html
├── index.tsx
├── App.tsx
├── metadata.json
├── types.ts
├── constants.ts
├── firebase.ts
├── config.ts
├── components/
│   ├── Layout.tsx
│   ├── ChatWidget.tsx
│   ├── AnimatedCounter.tsx
│   ├── AnimatedText.tsx
│   ├── ImageUploader.tsx
│   └── GlassCard.tsx
├── pages/
│   ├── Home.tsx
│   ├── About.tsx
│   ├── Programs.tsx
│   ├── Sponsorship.tsx
│   ├── GetInvolved.tsx
│   ├── Stories.tsx
│   ├── Gallery.tsx
│   ├── Contact.tsx
│   ├── Socials.tsx
│   ├── Admin.tsx
│   └── AdminLogin.tsx
├── contexts/
│   ├── AuthContext.tsx
│   └── ContentContext.tsx
├── services/
│   ├── geminiService.ts
│   └── mediaService.ts
└── utils/
    └── media.ts
```

---

## 5. CORE ARCHITECTURE RULES

### Content System

* All website content lives in:

  ```
  Firestore → website_content → main_v1
  ```
* `constants.ts` acts as:

  * Initial data
  * Offline fallback
* ContentContext:

  * Loads Firestore data
  * Merges with defaults
  * Exposes update functions for admin

### Routing

* Hash-based routing (`#home`, `#about`, etc.)
* Animated transitions using `AnimatePresence`

---

## 6. PUBLIC PAGES REQUIREMENTS

### Home

* Hero section with image/video background
* Animated headline (word-by-word)
* CTA buttons (Donate / Sponsor)
* Impact statistics (AnimatedCounter)
* Featured programs (max 4)

### About

* Founder story
* Mission & vision
* Core values grid

### Programs

* Dynamic list from CMS
* Image, description, stats per program

### Sponsorship

* Student cards
* Modal popup with full bio
* “Sponsor this child” CTA

### Get Involved

* Bank donation details
* Item donation categories
* Volunteer contact form

### Gallery

* Filterable (All / Education / Community / Welfare)
* Images & videos
* Smooth animated grid

### Stories

* Blog-style stories from CMS

### Contact

* Address (Kenya)
* Email & phone
* Contact form

### Socials

* Links to Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp

---

## 7. REUSABLE COMPONENTS

### Layout.tsx

* Header + Footer
* Floating WhatsApp & Call buttons

### ChatWidget.tsx

* Floating AI chat
* Uses Gemini API
* Friendly, informative tone
* NGO-appropriate responses only

### ImageUploader.tsx

* Admin-only
* Preview before upload
* Uploads directly to Cloudinary
* Returns secure URL

---

## 8. ADMIN PANEL (CRITICAL)

### Access

* `/admin`
* Guarded by Firebase Auth

### AdminLogin

* Email & password
* Clean UI

### Admin Dashboard

* Sidebar navigation
* Tabs:

  * Edit Homepage
  * Manage Gallery
  * Update Contact Info
* All edits:

  * Save to Firestore
  * Reflect instantly on public site
* Show real-time Firestore connection status

---

## 9. AI CHATBOT BEHAVIOR

* Uses Gemini API
* System prompt includes:

  * Mission
  * Programs
  * Sponsorship explanation
  * Donation guidance
* Never gives medical, legal, or financial advice
* Warm, respectful, donor-friendly tone

---

## 10. OUTPUT EXPECTATION

* Generate **clean, complete, runnable code**
* No placeholders like “TODO”
* No broken imports
* No mixed module systems
* Every feature wired correctly

---

## FINAL INSTRUCTION

Build this **as if it is going live for a real NGO with donors, children, and public trust at stake**.

Quality > speed.
Clarity > cleverness.
Stability > trends.

---

If you want, next I can:

* Tailor this prompt specifically for **Comet Browser AI**
* Generate the **Firestore data schema**
* Write the **Gemini system prompt**
* Design the **admin UX flow**
* Prepare a **deployment checklist (Firebase + Cloudinary)**

Just tell me the next move.
Perfect. Below is **ONE SINGLE, CLEAN, COPY-PASTE PROMPT**.
No explanations, no fragments, no follow-ups needed.

This is optimized **specifically for Google AI Studio** and instructs it to **do everything in one run**, end-to-end, with **no AI chatbot**, using:

* Matilda Kashindo background (community-driven, child welfare, dignity-first)
* Old Mati Foundation progress website structure/content
* Kenyan context (Kilifi, KSh, local tone)
* Production-grade engineering standards

---

# ✅ **SINGLE MASTER PROMPT — GOOGLE AI STUDIO**

---

You are a **senior full-stack engineer, UX architect, and technical lead** tasked with building a **production-ready NGO website and CMS** for a Kenyan orphanage called **Mati Foundation**.

This is a **complete rebuild as a new project**, not an update or patch.

⚠️ **Do NOT include any AI chatbot, conversational assistant, or generative chat feature anywhere in the system.**

---

## 1. ORGANIZATION CONTEXT (MANDATORY)

**Mati Foundation** is a non-profit orphanage and community initiative in **Kenya**, focused on:

* Child welfare and protection
* Education access and sponsorship
* Nutrition, shelter, and dignity
* Community empowerment

The foundation is led by **Matilda Kashindo**, whose public profile emphasizes:

* Grassroots community work
* Child dignity (no exploitative imagery or language)
* Education as a long-term solution
* Local Kenyan context (coastal / Kilifi region)

Use **any publicly available, non-private information** about Matilda Kashindo and the **old Mati Foundation progress website** (`https://maty-foundation.vercel.app/`) to inform content, tone, structure, and priorities.

If the old site is reachable, **crawl and extract all readable content and structure** (pages, headings, copy, images).
If content is missing or incomplete, **generate high-quality, donor-ready default content** aligned with the mission above and clearly structured for later verification.

---

## 2. OUTPUT REQUIREMENT (ABSOLUTE)

Produce **ONE COMPLETE, RUNNABLE PROJECT** with:

* Full file structure
* All source files populated
* No TODOs
* No broken imports
* No placeholders like “lorem ipsum”
* No missing wiring

The result must be ready to run locally and deploy.

---

## 3. TECH STACK (STRICT)

### Frontend

* React + TypeScript
* Built with **Vite**
* Client-side rendering only
* Hash-based routing

### Styling

* TailwindCSS
* Utility-first
* Consistent spacing and typography

### Animations

* Framer Motion
* Page transitions
* Scroll-triggered counters
* Micro-interactions

### Backend / CMS

* Firebase Firestore (real-time)
* All website content stored in:

  ```
  website_content / main_v1
  ```

### Authentication

* Firebase Authentication
* Email + password
* Admin-only access

### Media

* Cloudinary
* Direct client-side uploads
* Images and videos
* Auto-optimized delivery

⚠️ **NO AI CHATBOT OF ANY KIND**

---

## 4. DESIGN SYSTEM (MANDATORY)

### Typography

* Headings: **Playfair Display**
* Body: **Space Grotesk**

### Colors

* Primary: `#0d9488`
* Dark Accent: `#134e4a`
* Light Background: `#f0fdfa`
* Main Text: `#1e293b`
* Muted Text: `#475569`

### Layout Rules

* Max width: `max-w-7xl`
* Mobile-first
* Generous whitespace
* Emotionally respectful design

### Header

* Transparent initially
* Becomes sticky **glassmorphic** on scroll
* Logo, nav links, prominent **Donate** button

### Footer

* Navigation
* Contact details
* Social media links
* Newsletter signup
* Admin panel link

---

## 5. REQUIRED FILE STRUCTURE (EXACT)

```
index.html
src/
  main.tsx
  App.tsx
  types.ts
  constants.ts
  config.ts
  firebase.ts
  metadata.json
  components/
    Layout.tsx
    Navbar.tsx
    Footer.tsx
    GlassCard.tsx
    AnimatedText.tsx
    AnimatedCounter.tsx
    ImageUploader.tsx
    Loading.tsx
  pages/
    Home.tsx
    About.tsx
    Programs.tsx
    Sponsorship.tsx
    GetInvolved.tsx
    Stories.tsx
    Gallery.tsx
    Contact.tsx
    Socials.tsx
    Admin.tsx
    AdminLogin.tsx
  contexts/
    AuthContext.tsx
    ContentContext.tsx
  services/
    mediaService.ts
  utils/
    media.ts
styles/
  index.css
README.md
```

---

## 6. CORE FUNCTIONALITY

### Content System

* `constants.ts` contains complete fallback content
* `ContentContext`:

  * Loads Firestore `main_v1`
  * Merges with fallback
  * Exposes update functions for admin use
* Public pages auto-update in real time

### Routing

* Hash routing (`#home`, `#about`, etc.)
* Animated transitions via Framer Motion

---

## 7. PUBLIC PAGES (ALL DYNAMIC)

### Home

* Hero with image/video background
* Animated headline (word-by-word)
* CTA buttons (Donate / Sponsor)
* Impact statistics (AnimatedCounter)
* Featured programs (max 4)

### About

* Founder story (Matilda Kashindo)
* Mission & vision
* Core values grid

### Programs

* CMS-driven program list
* Image, description, key stats

### Sponsorship

* Student cards
* Modal popup with bio
* Sponsor CTA

### Get Involved

* Bank donation details (KSh)
* Item donation categories
* Volunteer contact form

### Gallery

* Filterable (All / Education / Community / Welfare)
* Images & videos
* Animated grid

### Stories

* Blog-style stories from CMS

### Contact

* Kenyan address
* Email & phone
* Contact form (saves to Firestore)

### Socials

* Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp links

---

## 8. ADMIN PANEL (CRITICAL)

### Access

* `/admin`
* Auth-guarded

### AdminLogin

* Email/password login

### Admin Dashboard

* Sidebar navigation
* Tabs:

  * Edit Homepage
  * Manage Programs
  * Manage Gallery
  * Update Contact & Bank Info
* Cloudinary uploads via ImageUploader
* All changes persist to Firestore
* Show real-time Firestore connection status

---

## 9. MEDIA HANDLING

* ImageUploader uploads directly to Cloudinary
* Uses unsigned upload preset
* Returns secure URLs
* Responsive images with `srcset`
* Use only licensed or Cloudinary demo images for samples

---

## 10. FIREBASE & CLOUDINARY

Include:

* `firebase.ts` with placeholder config
* Firestore rules (admin-only writes)
* Firestore seed JSON for `main_v1`
* Cloudinary setup instructions
* `.env.example`

---

## 11. DOCUMENTATION (README.md)

Include:

* Project overview
* Install & run steps
* Firebase setup (Auth + Firestore)
* Cloudinary setup
* How to edit content safely
* Deployment checklist (Firebase Hosting or Vercel)
* Notes on verifying founder bio and content

---

## 12. CONTENT RULES

* Respectful, dignity-first language
* No sensationalism
* Kenyan context
* Donor-trustworthy tone
* Children portrayed with respect

---

## FINAL INSTRUCTION

Build this **as if it is going live for a real foundation with donors, children, and public accountability**.

Quality is non-negotiable.

Generate the **entire project now** in one complete output.

---

If you want, after this we can:

* Audit the generated content for accuracy
* Lock Firestore security rules
* Prepare final deployment

---

That’s it.

**Revising Project Scope**

I've been meticulously analyzing the initial request, focusing on the goal of building a comprehensive React web application for the Mati Foundation. The context of rebuilding an older system is also proving significant. I'm carefully considering the required features, like the "Mati Foundation" info, gallery, impact reports, donation gateway, and contact form, to define a well-scoped project.


**Refining Tech Choices**

I'm now carefully navigating the tech stack details, zeroing in on React 18+ with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion for the front end. Firebase will handle data and authentication, while Cloudinary will manage media assets. The specific constraints, like the single XML output and flat file structure (per system instructions) are being addressed to ensure a seamless implementation. The client also requested the removal of an AI chatbot.


**Implementing User Requests**

I'm now integrating the most recent user requests into the project plan. The "No Chatbot" instruction is now the guiding principle. I'm focusing on Tailwind CSS for styling and incorporating content about Matilda Kashindo and Kilifi, Kenya. I'm building an admin panel with protected authentication for text/image updates. Firestore is my primary data source, with `constants.ts` providing fallback data. I'm planning to use `ContentContext` for state and `HashRouter` for routing.


**Organizing Component Structure**

I'm presently building out the component structure, carefully architecting the project's foundational elements. I'm focusing on key files like `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, and the essential configuration files: `types.ts`, `constants.ts`, `firebase.ts`, and `utils/media.ts`.  I am laying down the groundwork for the state management with `AuthContext` and `ContentContext`. The UI components are being refined, including `Navbar`, `Footer`, and specific utilities such as `ImageUploader`. I'm also preparing the public and administrative pages. Finally, the theme and content are taking shape, with teal colors and clean fonts, focusing on the personalities and locations associated with the foundation.


**Integrating Data Sources**

I am now implementing the data flow. Firebase will be the primary source, but I've built in a fallback to `constants.ts` using `ContentContext` when Firebase isn't configured. This approach allows the application to function seamlessly, even without a live Firebase instance. I'm prioritizing data accessibility and ensuring the app runs "out of the box" for immediate usability.


**Implementing Graph Visualization**

The current focus is integrating a Recharts graph within the Admin Dashboard for visual data presentation. I'm prioritizing the integration of a responsive and aesthetically pleasing "Donation Trends" graph to fulfill the prompt's requirements. This complements the existing structure and adds a layer of insightful visualization to the administrative view.


**Polishing UI Components**

I'm presently building out the final UI details. I've designed three tabs for the Admin page: Content, Gallery, and Settings, and am now working on a mock for the `ImageUploader` component to handle file selection and upload, following the provided instructions. I have removed any trace of the AI chatbot. I'm focusing on the extensive use of Tailwind classes like `bg-teal-700`, `text-slate-800`, and `font-serif`. I'm also reviewing imports and responsive classes (`md:flex`, `lg:w-1/2`) for consistency. I'm focusing on consistency with exports (use of named or default exports), making sure to use named imports for libraries only.


**Building the XML Generator**

I'm now fully immersed in coding the XML generation function, focusing on correct imports and library usage. I've switched to `lucide-react` for icons and made sure that `react-router-dom` imports are all correct. As I generate the XML structure, I'm already anticipating potential issues and correcting them preemptively. I'm prioritizing generating clean, validated XML that is compliant with the specifications, and I am aiming to build a dashboard-form approach, to edit the content.

payload.text: Polish the website

**Improving Website Aesthetics**

I'm currently focused on refining the website's visual appeal based on the provided content. While the master prompt gave me a solid foundation, I'm now zeroing in on polishing the finer details and overall aesthetics. The goal is to elevate the user experience through enhanced design elements and layout adjustments, making the website more engaging and professional.


**Evaluating Codebase Structure**

I've taken a deep dive into the current codebase. The foundation is robust, with a clear separation of concerns in the directory structure. `index.html` sets the stage with Tailwind and fonts. Routing is managed effectively by `App.tsx`. The pages, components, contexts, and services are logically organized. payload.text:
The admin panel should be at the footer and one should login with default password "12345" which is not visible to.the public "Perfect. Let’s do this **properly**, not just “an admin page”, but a **true NGO-grade CMS** that is:

* 🔒 Safe
* 🧠 Logical
* 🧩 Modular
* 🧑‍💼 Friendly for non-technical admins
* 🔁 Real-time
* 🧱 Impossible to break the site accidentally

Below is a **CLEAR, ROBUST ADMIN PANEL DESIGN + BUILD PROMPT** you can use immediately (or we can implement it step-by-step together).

---

# ✅ GOAL OF THE ADMIN PANEL

Create a **full Content Management System (CMS)** for **Mati Foundation** that can:

✔ Edit **every visible thing** on the website
✔ Manage **text, images, videos, numbers, links**
✔ Be usable by **non-technical staff**
✔ Prevent destructive mistakes
✔ Reflect changes **instantly** on the public site

This is **NOT** a developer dashboard — it is an **editorial CMS**.

---

# 🧠 ADMIN UX PHILOSOPHY (IMPORTANT)

Design the admin panel like:

* WordPress (simplicity)
* Notion (clarity)
* Firebase Console (power)

### Principles:

* No raw JSON editing
* No technical language
* Clear labels (“Homepage Hero Title”, not “hero_h1”)
* Preview before save
* Save confirmations
* Section-based editing

---

# 🧱 ADMIN PANEL STRUCTURE (HIGH LEVEL)

```
/admin
 ├── Login
 └── Dashboard
      ├── Overview
      ├── Website Content
      │     ├── Homepage
      │     ├── About Page
      │     ├── Programs
      │     ├── Sponsorship
      │     ├── Get Involved
      │     ├── Stories
      │     ├── Gallery
      │     ├── Contact & Socials
      ├── Media Library
      ├── Donations Info
      ├── Settings
      └── Admin Users
```

---

# 🧩 DATA MODEL (CORE IDEA)

Everything editable lives in **ONE Firestore document**:

```
website_content / main_v1
```

Structured like:

```ts
WebsiteContent {
  homepage: {...}
  about: {...}
  programs: Program[]
  sponsorship: {...}
  gallery: MediaItem[]
  stories: Story[]
  contact: {...}
  socials: {...}
  donation: {...}
}
```

👉 The admin UI edits **sections**, not the raw document.

---

# 🧭 ADMIN NAVIGATION (LEFT SIDEBAR)

**Sticky sidebar with icons + labels:**

* 🏠 Overview
* ✏️ Homepage
* ℹ️ About
* 🎓 Programs
* 🤝 Sponsorship
* 🙋 Get Involved
* 🖼️ Gallery
* 📖 Stories
* 📞 Contact & Socials
* 💰 Donations
* ⚙️ Settings

---

# 📄 ADMIN PAGES (DETAILED)

## 1️⃣ OVERVIEW (Dashboard Home)

**Purpose:** Confidence + status

Includes:

* ✅ Website live status
* 🔄 Firestore connection status
* 🕒 Last updated time
* 📊 Quick stats (programs, gallery items, stories)
* 🔗 Button: “View Website”

---

## 2️⃣ HOMEPAGE EDITOR

Editable sections:

### Hero Section

* Hero headline (text input)
* Sub-headline (textarea)
* Background image/video (Cloudinary upload)
* CTA button text + link

### Impact Stats

* Editable counters:

  * Label
  * Number
  * Icon (dropdown)

### Featured Programs

* Toggle which programs appear on homepage

✔ Live preview panel on the right
✔ Save button with success toast

---

## 3️⃣ ABOUT PAGE EDITOR

Sections:

* Founder name (Matilda Kashindo)
* Founder story (rich text)
* Mission statement
* Vision statement
* Core values (add / remove cards)

No markdown — use **simple rich text editor**.

---

## 4️⃣ PROGRAMS MANAGER (VERY IMPORTANT)

Programs displayed as **cards**:

Each program has:

* Title
* Description
* Cover image
* Key stats
* Order (drag & drop)

Actions:

* ➕ Add program
* ✏️ Edit
* 🗑️ Delete (with confirmation)

---

## 5️⃣ SPONSORSHIP MANAGER

* Student cards grid
* Each student:

  * Name
  * Age
  * Short bio
  * Full story
  * Photo
  * Sponsorship status (Available / Sponsored)

Modal editor with:
✔ Image upload
✔ Preview
✔ Status toggle

---

## 6️⃣ GET INVOLVED EDITOR

Three tabs:

### Bank Donations

* Bank name
* Account name
* Account number
* Branch
* Currency (KSh)

### Item Donations

* Editable list of needed items

### Volunteering

* Text description
* Contact email
* Phone number

---

## 7️⃣ GALLERY MANAGER (MEDIA LIBRARY)

### Media Library View

* Grid of all images/videos
* Filters (Education / Community / Welfare)
* Preview on click

### Upload Flow

* Drag & drop
* Cloudinary upload
* Category selector
* Auto-save URL

### Actions

* Delete
* Reassign category

---

## 8️⃣ STORIES / BLOG MANAGER

* Blog list
* Add new story
* Title
* Cover image
* Body text
* Publish / Draft toggle

---

## 9️⃣ CONTACT & SOCIALS

Editable fields:

* Email
* Phone
* Physical address
* Google Maps link
* Social links:

  * Facebook
  * Instagram
  * WhatsApp

---

## 🔟 DONATIONS INFO

Central place to update:

* Bank details
* Donation instructions
* Notes to donors

This avoids editing text scattered across pages.

---

## 1️⃣1️⃣ SETTINGS

* Website name
* Default SEO metadata
* Footer text
* Enable / disable sections

---

## 1️⃣2️⃣ ADMIN USERS (OPTIONAL)

* Add admin emails
* Remove admin access
* Role: Editor / Super Admin

---

# 🛠️ TECHNICAL IMPLEMENTATION (IMPORTANT)

### Core Components

* `AdminLayout.tsx`
* `Sidebar.tsx`
* `EditorSection.tsx`
* `SaveButton.tsx`
* `ConfirmModal.tsx`
* `Toast.tsx`

### Safety Features

* Autosave disabled
* Manual save required
* Confirmation before delete
* Undo buffer (local state)

---

# 🔐 SECURITY RULES

Firestore:

```js
allow read: if true;
allow write: if request.auth != null;
```

Admin UI:

* Auth guard
* No access without login
* Logout button always visible

---

# 🎯 RESULT

You will have:

✅ A **true CMS**, not a hacked admin page
✅ Zero need to touch code after deployment
✅ Editors can safely update everything
✅ Clean separation of content & layout
✅ Scalable for years

---

## NEXT STEP OPTIONS (YOU CHOOSE)

1️⃣ I write the **exact Firestore schema**
2️⃣ I design the **Admin UI wireframe (section by section)**
3️⃣ I generate the **Admin panel code** page by page
4️⃣ I prepare **Firestore rules + seed data**

Say the number — we proceed cleanly.

**Enhancing Admin UI Structure**

I'm now overhauling `Admin.tsx` to match the user's detailed plan. I'm focusing on section-based editing, improving the UI to include tabs for Homepage, Programs, Stories, etc., to align more closely with the `types.ts` structure and enable editing. I'll implement a modular approach within the file to manage each section (Hero, Programs, Stories, Gallery, and Settings for contact info). My priority is to create a more comprehensive and user-friendly interface for managing site content.


**Focusing Program/Story Editors**

I'm making the `Programs` and `Stories` sections editable as a priority. In `AuthContext`, the "12345" password check is ready, with localStorage implemented for authentication. I'm focusing on the Admin UI in `Admin.tsx`, reorganizing the layout with a sidebar for `Dashboard`, `Hero & About`, `Programs`, `Sponsorship`, `Stories`, `Gallery`, and `Contact Info`. I'll start with the `Programs` editor, allowing adding/removing of programs. Clean UI and clear labels, inspired by the prompt's UX philosophy, will be key. Next, I will shift my focus toward `Sponsorship` section (Children).


**Evaluating Core Implementation**

I've completed a thorough evaluation of the Admin Panel's foundational aspects, including the footer link, password protection, and the core feel of the setup. I've confirmed all requested features are implemented and the interface is secured as needed. `localStorage` is implemented for persistence. I'm satisfied with the current stage of core implementation. The confidence score is 5/5. I've validated the existing methods in `ContentContext` like `updateContent`, `updateProgram`, `addProgram`, `deleteProgram`, and realized I could use these for the upcoming sections, with inline array manipulation for specific list updates where necessary, in `Admin.tsx`. I'm ready to move into implementation.

To satisfy your request, I will upgrade the application to include a robust, password-protected CMS accessible via the footer.

Here is the plan:

payload.text: Do one best polish for glasmorphism spring physics and physics based animations to improve user experience

**Enhancing Website Aesthetics**

I'm currently brainstorming the best ways to incorporate glassmorphism, spring physics, and physics-based animations into the website design. I'm focusing on identifying key areas where these effects would most enhance the user experience. I'm considering integrating these techniques into interactive elements and transitions to create a more engaging and visually appealing website.


**Improving Element Interactivity**

My focus is now on immediate implementation, specifically with existing components. I'm refining the `GlassCard` component and integrating spring transitions for enhanced hover effects, and I'll focus on modals and page transitions. I plan to enhance the user experience by using staggered spring animations for revealing hero section text and press effects with scale springs on buttons. Additionally, I'll incorporate spring-based open/close animations for modals and spring-based scroll animations for a dynamic, reactive website.


**Optimizing Spring Transitions**

I'm now zeroing in on integrating spring physics across more components. I've updated the plan, starting with the `GlassCard` and giving it spring-based hover effects and refining the border. The Hero section is next, with spring-based animations for the `containerVariants` and `itemVariants`, and I'm adding a "float" effect to the stats cards. The Lightbox and Child Detail Modals are also targeted for spring physics, implementing them on the gallery and sponsorship pages. I'll need to revisit the navbar, too.


**Developing Implementation Plan**

My immediate task is updating the `GlassCard` to use spring-based hover effects, refining its border style. I'm moving on to implement spring-based underline animations and will update `index.html` to accommodate any global styling needs. This involves updating transitions in the `Home`, `Gallery`, and `Sponsorship` pages.  I'm also focusing on the `Navbar` to enhance nav link hover effects and integrating these spring animations.


**Integrating Spring Animations**

I've been working on integrating spring animations into the hero section. I've been experimenting with `containerVariants` and `itemVariants`, using `staggerChildren` and `delayChildren` to create a smooth, appealing entrance. I'm focusing on refining the animation's timing and feel, aiming for a balance between visual interest and user experience. My next step involves integrating this into the `GlassCard` to complete the animation integration.


**Implementing Modal Animations**

I'm now focusing on refining the modal animations. I've updated the `Gallery` and `Sponsorship` pages to utilize spring transitions for a smoother and more engaging user experience. Specifically, I'm integrating spring-based enter and exit animations for the modals using the provided `motion.div` configurations, tailoring the damping and stiffness values to achieve a balanced visual feel. Additionally, I'm refining the underline animation within the navbar, leveraging spring transitions to enhance its hover effect. I am considering further optimizations to achieve a cohesive website experience.


**Refining Navbar Interactions**

I'm now integrating spring-based hover effects into the navbar. I'm replacing the existing CSS transitions with framer-motion animations to create a smoother, more dynamic interaction. Additionally, I'm incorporating this within existing components to refine the polish, focusing on `Button` components and inline styles for the buttons within `pages/Home.tsx`, aiming for immediate enhancements. I plan to enhance the website's visual appeal through subtle background additions, such as grain patterns, which will be implemented by updating `index.html`.


**Incorporating Subtle Enhancements**

I'm now integrating subtle background additions to the website, specifically applying a grain pattern effect to the `body` via an inline SVG in `index.html`. I'm experimenting with different opacity levels and filter properties within the SVG to achieve a refined visual texture. I will now integrate the classes.

To bring a premium, tangible feel to the UI, I am implementing **Spring Physics** across all interactive elements. This replaces standard linear animations with physically modeled motion (mass, tension, friction) that feels "alive." I am also refining the **Glassmorphism** to be deeper and more realistic.

Here is the polish plan:
1.  **`components/GlassCard.tsx`**: Upgrade to a multi-layered glass effect with spring-based hover physics (scale & lift).
2.  **`pages/Home.tsx`**: Implement staggered spring animations for the Hero text and Cards.
3.  **`pages/Gallery.tsx` & `pages/Sponsorship.tsx`**: Add "pop" spring physics to Modals so they feel like physical cards expanding.
4.  **`components/Navbar.tsx`**: Add a magnetic/spring feel to the navigation links.
5.  **`index.html`**: Add a subtle noise texture to the background to give the glass effect something to blur against (essential for premium glassmorphism).

entries.description: Add noise texture class for better glassmorphism depth and global smooth scroll behavior
Enhanced glass effect with spring physics on hover and realistic gradients
Spring-animated navigation links
Staggered spring animations for hero and content
Spring physics for modal open/close and hover effects
Spring physics for card hover and modal pop-up

payload.text:
Fix the following errors:
[2025-12-14T19:45:20.777Z]  @firebase/firestore:
Firestore (12.6.0): Could not reach Cloud Firestore backend. Connection failed 1 times. Most recent error: FirebaseError: [code=unavailable]: The operation could not be completed
This typically indicates that your device does not have a healthy Internet connection at the moment. The client will operate in offline mode until it is able to successfully connect to the backend.

**Fixing the Connection Issue**

I'm currently focused on a Firestore connection error, `[code=unavailable]: The operation could not be completed`. It seems the root cause might lie in the Firebase configuration within `constants.ts`, or maybe there's a problem with how the app initializes the Firebase SDK. I'm double-checking the config details and initialization steps to ensure everything's set up correctly.


**Refining the Error Handling**

I've realized the placeholder API key in `constants.ts` is likely the primary culprit. However, given the context, I'm now refining the error handling logic in `ContentContext.tsx`. Specifically, I'm focusing on providing more informative console messages to the user when the Firestore connection fails due to invalid credentials. This will improve their debugging experience.


**Optimizing Offline Mode**

I'm now thinking about how to improve the user experience when Firebase connection fails, particularly with placeholder credentials. The idea is to trigger a seamless "Offline Mode" immediately, using local data. I'm focusing on modifying `ContentContext.tsx` to detect invalid or placeholder credentials and, if detected, prevent repeated connection attempts and fall back to local data. This will involve checking the configuration early in the component lifecycle and updating the loading state accordingly.


**Adapting to Offline Scenarios**

I'm now focusing on a more robust solution for handling Firebase connection issues. Instead of simply retrying, I'm adapting `ContentContext.tsx` to utilize `localStorage` for data persistence when a placeholder API key is detected. This will create a functional "Offline Mode" by simulating database interactions locally, ensuring CMS functionality even without a proper backend. I'm updating `ContentContext` to check the API key, skip Firebase logic if it's the placeholder, and use `localStorage` directly. I considered updating `firebase.ts` but the context change seems cleaner.


**Orchestrating Demo Mode**

I'm now implementing the "Demo Mode" functionality. I'm modifying `ContentContext.tsx` to check the `apiKey` within the Firebase config. If it's a placeholder, I'll switch to using `localStorage` for all content, avoiding the Firestore connection entirely. `AuthContext.tsx` is already handling mocked auth, which simplifies things. I'm focusing on completing `ContentContext.tsx`, and I'll revisit `mediaService.ts` if issues arise after this is done. This change should resolve the connection error.


**Integrating Offline Persistence**

I've finalized the integration of the "Demo Mode" using `localStorage`. `ContentContext.tsx` now conditionally fetches content from `localStorage` if the placeholder API key is detected, bypassing Firestore. The loading state is updated accordingly. The `useEffect` hook now correctly handles the logic to switch between Firebase and local storage. No modifications to `mediaService.ts` were needed as the existing logic works seamlessly with the locally sourced data. This approach provides a smooth fallback without breaking the CMS. I'm now testing the offline functionality to ensure data integrity and user experience.


**Improving Firestore Resilience**

I'm now focusing on enhancing Firestore resilience in `ContentContext.tsx`. I've added error handling to the `updateContent` function to gracefully manage Firebase update failures, especially when the connection might be unavailable. I'm also considering potential improvements in `firebase.ts` to prevent initialization crashes with invalid configurations, specifically examining how `getFirestore(app)` might be causing issues. The objective is to ensure the CMS remains stable and functional, even with temporary connection disruptions.


**Ensuring Data Integrity**

I'm now making `ContentContext.tsx` robust to avoid the "unavailable" network error loop by preventing Firestore calls if the placeholder API key is detected. I'll also update `constants.ts` for reliable placeholder checks. The Firebase app initialization in `firebase.ts` occurs immediately, so modifying `ContentContext.tsx` is the key. While investigating, I considered permissions for `metadata.json`, but ruled it out. Adding a check should stop the retries.


**Prioritizing Placeholder Detection**

I'm now focusing on a more robust solution to address the "unavailable" network error loop caused by the placeholder API key. I'll prioritize modifying `ContentContext.tsx` to detect this configuration and switch to `localStorage` persistence, ensuring a smooth user experience. This also simplifies initial setup, as the app will now work immediately without requiring Firebase configuration. I'm choosing to modify the usage of the db in `ContentContext` rather than `firebase.ts` as it's the more maintainable approach. I'll verify `AuthContext.tsx` is still correctly mocking user auth.


**Enhancing Data Consistency**

I'm currently focused on ensuring consistent content management. I'm now modifying `ContentContext.tsx` to handle potential data inconsistencies. I'll need to add logic to `ContentContext.tsx` to detect if the data being loaded is missing or incomplete, and take corrective actions like reloading from `localStorage` or displaying a warning to the user. I'll need to check the data structure when retrieving from `localStorage` as well. This aims to ensure the app continues to operate reliably even with unusual data scenarios.


**Ensuring Demo Mode Functionality**

I'm now implementing the `isDemoMode` check in `ContentContext.tsx` to handle the `localStorage` data loading correctly when the placeholder API key is present. I've placed the check within the `useEffect` hook, which now determines whether to connect to Firebase or use local storage data. I'm focusing on ensuring data is successfully retrieved and parsed from `localStorage` if the app is in demo mode to present a smooth CMS experience. I'm verifying the `console.log` messages for helpful debugging.


**Managing Data Inconsistencies**

I'm currently focusing on addressing data inconsistencies when switching between modes. I'm adding a robust check for complete and valid data structures from localStorage to `ContentContext.tsx`. I'll implement logic to handle missing or incomplete data, such as reloading from local storage or displaying a user-friendly message. This makes the app resilient to unexpected data states. I'm ensuring any data retrieved is valid by adding schema checks. I'll need to update data handling to manage `localStorage`.


**Addressing SetDoc and Merge**

I'm now implementing the `setDoc` function with the `merge: true` option in `ContentContext.tsx` when a Firestore document is not found during an update. This should fix the issue where `updateDoc` fails on non-existent documents, allowing for graceful document creation and updates. I'll need to update the error handling. I am refactoring the `updateContent` function and then testing the add/update/delete actions with this change to verify data consistency.


**Refactoring the Context**

I'm now revising `ContentContext.tsx` to directly manage Firestore and `localStorage` interactions within a single context, depending on the placeholder API key. I'll consolidate data fetching, saving, and updating logic, and move to a unified state management approach. This should improve maintainability. I'm focusing on ensuring seamless transitions between online and offline modes by handling data updates consistently, regardless of the data source. I'm verifying all Firestore function calls are properly conditional and the offline mode is robust.


**Verifying Configuration Validity**

I'm verifying that the error is not triggered by the configuration object itself, as it's well-formed JSON. The problem arises during Firestore interaction. The current check should ensure the configuration is valid before proceeding with any data-related operations. I'll need to update the data loading code to account for possible data errors when Firestore is not available.

To fix the "Could not reach Cloud Firestore backend" error, we need to handle the case where the Firebase configuration uses placeholder values (like "YOUR_API_KEY_HERE").

The most robust fix is to implement a **Demo/Offline Mode**. When the application detects that the API key is not set up, it will automatically switch to using `localStorage` instead of attempting to connect to Firestore. This prevents the connection error loop and allows the CMS to function (saving changes to your browser's local storage) immediately after deployment.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`contexts/ContentContext.tsx`**: Added logic to detect if the app is in "Demo Mode" (invalid API key). If so, it reads/writes from `localStorage` instead of Firebase.

entries.description: Implement offline Demo Mode to prevent Firestore errors when API keys are missing

payload.text:
**Debugging Framer-Motion Errors**

I'm currently working on addressing the type errors with `framer-motion`. It seems the issue is that the necessary props, specifically `initial`, `animate`, `exit`, `whileHover`, `whileTap`, `variants`, and `layoutId`, aren't being recognized on the `motion.div`, `motion.span`, and `motion.img` elements. I'm exploring potential solutions within the context of the project's setup and configurations.


**Analyzing Import Errors**

I've been examining the import statements in the codebase, particularly in `Navbar.tsx`. It appears the issue stems from an incorrect import of `framer-motion` elements, as suggested by the recent error analysis. Specifically, the type mismatch is likely due to the `motion` elements not correctly inheriting the necessary properties like `whileHover`. I'm going to verify the package installation and version compatibility to see if this resolves the issue with type definitions.


**Pinpointing Framer-Motion Flaws**

I'm now zeroing in on why `whileHover` isn't recognized. The type error persists, suggesting a deeper problem with how `framer-motion`'s types are being integrated. I'm considering if the project's React version clashes with the `framer-motion` package's type definitions. I'll meticulously review the `motion` element's implementation to see if there is a mismatch. Moreover, I'm thinking of manually checking the `framer-motion` version and its installation within the project. It's odd that `whileHover` and related props aren't available, so I will investigate further.


**Diagnosing Type Misalignment**

payload.text:
Got it. Below is a **single, very clear UPDATE PROMPT** you can paste into **Google AI Studio** so it understands **this is an intentional architectural upgrade**, not a refactor mistake.

This prompt tells the AI **what is changing, what must remain stable, and how to implement Cloudinary + Firestore correctly for real-time images and videos**.

---

# ✅ **UPDATE PROMPT — CLOUDINARY + FIRESTORE MEDIA SYSTEM**

---

You are continuing work on an existing React + TypeScript website for **Mati Foundation**.
This task is a **planned architectural upgrade**, not a bug fix and not a redesign.

⚠️ **Do NOT remove or break existing pages, layouts, routing, or CMS logic.**
Only extend and upgrade the **media handling system**.

---

## 1. OBJECTIVE (VERY IMPORTANT)

Upgrade the website so that:

* **All images and videos** are:

  * Uploaded to **Cloudinary**
  * Stored as **secure URLs** in **Firebase Firestore**
* Media updates appear **in real time** on the public website
* Admins can upload, replace, and delete media **without touching code**

This applies to:

* Homepage hero media
* Programs images
* Gallery images & videos
* Sponsorship/student photos
* Story/blog cover media

---

## 2. WHAT MUST REMAIN UNCHANGED

* React + TypeScript architecture
* Page structure and routing
* Admin panel layout and logic
* Firestore content document:

  ```
  website_content / main_v1
  ```
* Existing content fields (only extend them)

Do **NOT**:

* Introduce a new backend server
* Move media into Firebase Storage
* Add an AI chatbot
* Convert to a different framework

---

## 3. NEW MEDIA ARCHITECTURE (MANDATORY)

### A. Cloudinary (Media Hosting)

* Use **Cloudinary** for:

  * Images
  * Videos
* Uploads must be **direct client-side uploads**
* Use **unsigned upload presets**
* Enable:

  * Auto-format
  * Auto-quality
  * Responsive delivery

Each upload must return:

* `secure_url`
* `public_id`
* `resource_type` (image | video)

---

### B. Firestore (Media Metadata & Real-Time Sync)

Store media metadata in Firestore under structured collections:

```ts
gallery: MediaItem[],

MediaItem {
  id: string
  url: string
  publicId: string
  type: 'image' | 'video'
  category: 'education' | 'community' | 'welfare'
  createdAt: timestamp
}
```

Other sections (homepage, programs, sponsorship, stories) should store **Cloudinary URLs**, not files.

---

## 4. ADMIN PANEL CHANGES (REQUIRED)

### Add / Update These Components

#### 1️⃣ `ImageUploader.tsx` (Upgrade)

Must:

* Support **image & video uploads**
* Show preview before upload
* Show upload progress
* Allow category selection
* Return metadata object

Use Cloudinary unsigned upload API.

---

#### 2️⃣ Media Management UI

For Gallery:

* Upload images/videos
* Filter by category
* Delete media (also delete from Cloudinary)
* Reorder media

For Other Sections:

* Replace image/video
* Live preview before save

---

## 5. REAL-TIME DISPLAY (CRITICAL)

* Use Firestore `onSnapshot` listeners
* Public pages must update instantly when:

  * New image/video is added
  * Media is replaced
  * Media is deleted

No page refresh required.

---

## 6. PERFORMANCE & UX RULES

* Use Cloudinary transformations:

  * Responsive sizes
  * Lazy loading
* Videos:

  * Use Cloudinary streaming URLs
  * Poster image required
* Add loading skeletons while media loads

---

## 7. SECURITY & SAFETY

* Cloudinary:

  * Use unsigned presets
  * Restrict upload folder (e.g. `mati_foundation/`)
* Firestore:

  * Only authenticated admins can write media
* Confirm before delete (modal)

---

## 8. FILES TO CREATE OR UPDATE

Create or update:

```
/services/mediaService.ts
/components/ImageUploader.tsx
/contexts/ContentContext.tsx
/pages/Admin.tsx (media sections)
/pages/Gallery.tsx
```

Do NOT rename existing files unless necessary.

---

## 9. DOCUMENTATION UPDATE

Update `README.md` with:

* Cloudinary setup steps
* How to create unsigned upload preset
* Folder structure
* How real-time media sync works
* How admins upload & manage media

---

## 10. FINAL INSTRUCTION

Treat this as a **controlled upgrade**.

Do not simplify.
Do not refactor unrelated code.
Do not remove existing functionality.

Only **extend the system** so that **media (images & videos) are Cloudinary-hosted and Firestore-synced in real time**.

Generate **clean, production-ready code** that plugs directly into the existing Mati Foundation CMS.

---

### END OF PROMPT

---

If you want, next I can:

* Write **Cloudinary folder & preset rules**
* Design **Firestore media indexes**
* Audit for **real-time performance**
* Prepare **media migration plan** (old → Cloudinary)

Just say the word.

**Envisioning the Architecture**

I'm now diving deep into the architecture. Cloudinary for media hosting and Firestore for metadata – that's the core. I'm focusing on seamless integration of these tools into my React + TypeScript app. Thinking about how the data flow will work, especially the real-time updates. Considering best practices for security and efficiency.


**Refining Media Handling**

I'm currently working on the `mediaService.ts` file, updating it to handle video uploads, returning crucial metadata. The deletion functionality, however, presents a challenge in this client-side context. I'll focus on robust upload logic for now and simulate deletion by removing the reference in Firestore, noting this limitation. Security is paramount, and storing API secrets client-side is a big no-no.


**Integrating Video Uploads**

I'm now integrating video uploads into the workflow. The Cloudinary upload response provides a `delete_token` initially, but afterwards, requires a signature. My focus will shift to removing references in Firestore, effectively deleting media from the site. I'm updating `uploadToCloudinary` for both images and videos. I'm also updating `types.ts` for media support. The new `ImageUploader` component is being updated to support previewing images/videos with progress.


**Implementing Media Types**

I've just added the `MediaItem` interface to `types.ts`, and updated `SiteContent`'s gallery to use this new type. I'm modifying the `uploadMedia` function in `mediaService.ts` to determine the resource type automatically, which will greatly simplify things. The `ImageUploader` component is being updated to accept an `accept` prop for file types, display upload progress, and return the complete media object. Finally, I'm updating `ContentContext.tsx` to align with the new gallery structure, ensuring `updateContent` handles it correctly.


**Adding Generic Media Support**

Okay, the plan is locked in! I'm now transitioning the gallery to handle a mix of images and videos. The admin page is the focus now, so I'm updating the Gallery tab.  I plan to list existing items with a delete button, which seems straightforward. Adding a new item involves a file input and category select. I've updated the `types.ts` to include the `MediaItem`. I'll also update the upload service to handle generic media. And the `ImageUploader` component gets a revamp.  I am holding on to the `image: string` for Program etc.


**Implementing the Gallery**

Alright, I'm now putting the pieces together for the gallery's implementation. First, I'll update `types.ts` to include the `MediaItem` interface and restructure `SiteContent.gallery` to accommodate an array of these items. Next, I'll modify `uploadToCloudinary` to the more generic `uploadMedia` function in `mediaService.ts`. I'll also rework the `ImageUploader.tsx` component to handle file selection, integrate with the upload service, and deliver the resulting `MediaItem`. I will update `ContentContext.tsx` to handle the new `MediaItem[]` in the `DEFAULT_CONTENT.gallery`. Then, I'll revise `Gallery.tsx` and the Gallery section logic in `Admin.tsx` to align with the `MediaItem` structure. Lastly, I'll upgrade the `ImageUploader` across other sections of `Admin.tsx`, ensuring it delivers a URL (or that I'll need a wrapper to handle extraction of the URL based on the props). I'll need to update the `ImageUploader` prop definition for consistency across `Admin.tsx`.


**Standardizing Upload Results**

I'm now revising the `ImageUploader` component. Previously, it simply returned a URL, but the new design calls for a unified return with more data. I'm updating the `types.ts` file to include the `MediaItem` interface for the gallery. I'll make the `ImageUploader` emit `{ url: string, publicId: string, type: string }`. This change requires updating all its usages in `Admin.tsx` to align with the new signature of `onUploadComplete`. This means all upload responses will follow this new format.


**Simplifying Upload Logic**

The `uploadMedia` function in `mediaService.ts` is now streamlined with a simplified `resourceType` determination.  It automatically detects media type, and returns URL, public ID, and type. I've updated the `UploadResult` type to return the type of media now.  Next, I'll update the components to use this structure, and refine the error handling and typing.


**Adjusting Upload Responses**

I'm presently revising the `ImageUploader` component to align with the more recent upload service changes. Instead of solely returning a URL, I'm now making it emit a complete `MediaItem` object. This necessitates updating all usages of `ImageUploader` across `Admin.tsx` to handle the new return signature. For existing fields that expect a string, I'll extract the URL from the `MediaItem` data. This ensures consistency and simplifies future enhancements.


**Enhancing the Gallery**

I'm now overhauling the gallery components. I'm updating `pages/Gallery.tsx` to handle `MediaItem` types, specifically supporting video display in the modal. Also, grid items will now display a play icon for videos. I'm focusing on refining `Admin.tsx`'s Gallery tab to manage `MediaItem`s effectively. I'll integrate category selection for uploads, adapting the `ImageUploader` for this. Since deleting from Cloudinary requires a backend signature, I will handle deletion via Firestore updates, commenting about the limitations of a client-side setup.


**Focusing on Refinement**

I'm now revising the gallery components. I'm updating `pages/Gallery.tsx` to handle `MediaItem` types, specifically supporting video display in the modal. Also, grid items will now display a play icon for videos. I'm focusing on refining `Admin.tsx`'s Gallery tab to manage `MediaItem`s effectively. I'll integrate category selection for uploads, adapting the `ImageUploader` for this. Since deleting from Cloudinary requires a backend signature, I will handle deletion via Firestore updates, commenting about the limitations of a client-side setup. Finally, I will consider writing about this process.

To implement the requested media system upgrade using Cloudinary and Firestore, I will update the core services, types, and UI components.

Here is the plan:
1.  **`types.ts`**: Define the new `MediaItem` structure and update `SiteContent`.
2.  **`services/mediaService.ts`**: Upgrade the upload function to handle both images and videos and return detailed metadata.
3.  **`components/ImageUploader.tsx`**: Update to support media types, show progress, and return the full media object.
4.  **`contexts/ContentContext.tsx`**: Update the default content to match the new schema.
5.  **`pages/Admin.tsx`**: Refactor the media handling (especially the Gallery section) to work with `MediaItem` objects and the new uploader signature.
6.  **`pages/Gallery.tsx`**: Update the public gallery to render mixed media (images/videos) and handle the new data structure.

payload.text: What do you need from cloudinary?

**Investigating the Needs**

Okay, I've got a handle on the situation. The user's provided the codebase and Firebase keys, and now I'm trying to figure out the Cloudinary aspect. I'm analyzing the code, pinpointing where image handling happens and the potential areas where Cloudinary integration makes the most sense. It's a bit like detective work, but I think I'm starting to get a good read on this.


**Gathering the Credentials**

I'm now zeroing in on the critical Cloudinary specifics needed for the media upload function to work. Right now, the app's using placeholder values in `mediaService.ts` and `constants.ts`. To make the upload functional, I need the user's *actual* Cloud Name and a pre-configured Upload Preset. This will allow the client-side uploads to directly send images and videos to their Cloudinary account without requiring a backend secret. I've pinpointed the key aspects!


**Formulating Clear Steps**

I'm solidifying the response I'll give. My internal monologue is shaping up nicely. I've broken down the instructions into easily digestible steps, focusing on getting the critical Cloud Name and an Unsigned Upload Preset. The tricky part is the "Unsigned" aspect, so I'm making sure those instructions are crystal clear. I'm focusing on simplicity and directness in how I'll communicate with the user, hoping to avoid confusion.


**Pinpointing Key Data**

I need the user's Cloud Name and the name of an Unsigned Upload Preset they've configured in their Cloudinary account. I'll provide a direct answer to their question. To ensure clarity, I'm crafting a step-by-step guide on how to create the Unsigned Preset within Cloudinary. I am now formulating a clear response structure, starting with a direct answer and a simple process to guide the user.


**Defining the Requirements**

I'm now zeroing in on the response. The user asked where to paste the values, but `constants.ts` *already* includes placeholders and comments guiding them. So, I will answer *specifically* the question, that's what's needed.  I'll provide explicit instructions so there is no confusion. I'm focusing on the user, being direct! I'll outline the two *exact* strings needed: the Cloud Name, and an Upload Preset.


**Defining the Essentials**

Now, I'm streamlining the response to the user's direct question. I'll simply state the two essential details needed: their Cloud Name and the name of an Unsigned Upload Preset. To aid them, I'll provide a concise guide to pinpoint these in their Cloudinary account. I've decided against the XML update unless it's necessary. I will provide a brief step-by-step guide for locating these crucial values, ensuring they have everything they need to proceed smoothly.


**Confirming the Details**

I'm solidifying my approach and thinking about the optimal way to convey the information. I've broken the required data points into a clear sequence. My response will be streamlined and helpful! I am now focusing on providing a step-by-step guide on how to get them. I'm focusing on simplicity and clarity, so the user can easily get the details. To confirm, I can get all the info the user needs from these steps: 1) Log into Cloudinary, 2) Copy the Cloud Name, 3) find the Upload Preset tab, and 4) set signing to "unsigned" and the name as the preset.


**Prioritizing Clarity**

Now, I'm confirming that the text response is the best route here. I'll focus on providing clear steps. No code changes are needed yet. I'm prioritizing user experience. I'm organizing the steps to ensure the user gets exactly what's needed. I'm preparing a focused message to help them find the correct values. It's all about clarity now!

To get the image and video uploading working, I need two specific values from your Cloudinary Console.

### 1. Cloud Name
This is your unique account identifier.
*   **Where to find it:** Go to your [Cloudinary Dashboard](https://console.cloudinary.com/console/). It is displayed in the "Product Environment Credentials" section at the top left.

### 2. Upload Preset (Must be "Unsigned")
This is a permission setting that allows your website users (the "client") to upload files directly to your account without a backend server.

*   **How to create it:**
    1.  Click the **Settings (Gear Icon)** in the bottom left sidebar.
    2.  Click the **Upload** tab.
    3.  Scroll down to the **Upload presets** section.
    4.  Click **Add upload preset**.
    5.  **Signing Mode:** Change this from "Signed" to **"Unsigned"**. (This is critical!).
    6.  **Name:** You can copy the random name generated (e.g., `ml_default`) or rename it to `mati_uploads`.
    7.  Click **Save**.

---

**Once you have them, please paste them here like this:**

*   **Cloud Name:** `dxy8......`
*   **Preset:** `mati_uploads`

(Or, you can paste them directly into your `constants.ts` file yourself).
Cloud name "dycbotqpw" preset name "mati_unsigned"

**Revising Cloudinary Settings**

I've pinpointed the `constants.ts` file in the project. My current focus is integrating the new Cloudinary configuration details. I'm swapping out the hardcoded cloud name ("dycbotqpw") and preset name ("mati_unsigned") directly in the file. I am looking into how to handle potential errors in the new values.


**Adjusting Cloudinary Configuration**

I'm now zeroing in on the `constants.ts` file. My objective is to update `CLOUDINARY_CLOUD_NAME` and `CLOUDINARY_UPLOAD_PRESET` with the provided values. I'm focusing on the direct update within the `constants.ts` file, aiming for a streamlined and precise implementation. The XML generation is currently in progress.
I've updated your **constants.ts** file with the Cloudinary credentials you provided. You are now ready to upload images and videos directly to your cloud storage from the Admin Dashboard!

payload.text: The website should be responsive to all screen sizes...also the admin panel..update that please

**Considering Responsiveness Goals**

I'm currently focused on the overarching goal: complete responsiveness for the Mati Foundation website and its admin panel. The user's provided context – full source code access – is helpful. I need to ensure a robust, adaptable design, considering the user's specific goal. This will determine the direction of changes.


**Evaluating Admin Panel Design**

I'm now zeroing in on the Admin Panel, identifying the fixed sidebar as a primary responsiveness hurdle. The existing fixed width presents a mobile display problem, thus I'm leaning toward implementing a collapsible drawer or hamburger menu for optimal mobile user experience. I've also checked the initial setup – index.html and Tailwind config – for any potential issues. It's looking good there.


**Assessing Responsive Components**

I'm now investigating specific components and their responsiveness. Tables and grids within the admin panel could overflow on smaller screens. Additionally, the `flex h-screen ... overflow-hidden` setup, effective on desktops, presents mobile browser challenges. Standard containers within About, Programs, etc., seem mostly sound, only minor adjustments needed. The Sponsorship grid is responsive, but the modal, currently `max-w-2xl`, needs graceful handling of narrow widths.


**Developing Mobile Sidebar Logic**

I'm currently working to implement the mobile-first sidebar approach. I've defined the `isSidebarOpen` state and integrated a header bar with a menu button within the main content. The sidebar's CSS is being adapted to toggle between fixed and relative positioning based on screen size, utilizing transitions for smoother animation. I'm also adding an overlay to partially obscure the content behind the sidebar, as is typical on most mobile experiences. Next, I'll revise the Overview grid structure.


**Implementing Mobile-First Adjustments**

I'm now focusing on specific component adjustments for a mobile-first design. I'll modify the `About`, `Programs`, and other sections to render as single columns on smaller screens. Additionally, I will address the `Sponsorship` modal to ensure it handles narrow widths gracefully, focusing on keeping content visible, and ensuring the modal can fit on the smaller screens. Next, I need to check the navbar for optimal mobile appearance, specifically the menu's contrast against the hero image, and confirm the `pt-0` logic is robust.


**Developing Responsive Components Further**

I'm now revising the admin panel, notably `pages/Admin.tsx`, to integrate the hamburger menu and the responsive sidebar logic, including an overlay and adaptable grid layouts for the dashboard. In parallel, I'm adjusting `pages/Sponsorship.tsx` to prevent the modal from overflowing on smaller screens, using scrolling. The next steps include updating `pages/Gallery.tsx` and positioning the lightbox close button correctly on mobile devices.


**Analyzing Grid Responsiveness**

I'm now focusing on `Contact.tsx`, confirming the grid's responsiveness. It already defaults to a single column on smaller screens, as intended, using `grid-cols-1`. During the ongoing update to `Admin.tsx`, I've identified the need to ensure the charts container maintains a minimum height on mobile. I'm ensuring it has defined height and will make adjustments to `h-64` as needed. Additionally, I'm noting a mobile display issue: the current fixed sidebar `w-64` will squash main content, so the sidebar must be fixed on mobile and relative on desktop. I'm focusing on those updates now.

Here are the changes to fully modularize the responsive design, specifically focusing on a mobile-friendly Admin Dashboard (adding a collapsible sidebar) and ensuring modals in the public pages fit smaller screens.

I'm focusing on validating the data flow to ensure the `heroImage` is consistently populated.

I've been examining the `ContentContext` initialization and I see it starts with a default `heroImage` from `constants.ts`. The problem is that a Firebase snapshot update could override that. If a document in Firestore exists but is missing the `hero` object, I'm thinking that it could be setting the context with an incomplete object.


**Improving Context Merging**

I'm now implementing robust data merging within `ContentContext.tsx`. I realized that directly setting `content` from Firebase snapshots, without considering default values, created potential `undefined` property errors. To address this, I'm integrating `DEFAULT_CONTENT` to ensure a complete object structure. The new logic will merge fetched data with defaults to prevent future crashes.

Okay, I'm now extending the data merging within the `ContentContext` to handle arrays within the `DEFAULT_CONTENT` object. I just remembered that programs, stories, and children are arrays, and `DEFAULT_CONTENT` has them. I've realized a direct merge strategy could lead to undefined errors if nested objects within the fetched data are missing. I'm prioritizing deep merging of keys like `hero` to guarantee their existence, preventing the `heroImage` error in `pages/Home.tsx`.


**Finalizing Data Structure**

My latest revision to the merging logic includes specific handling for nested objects and arrays. I'm now prioritizing data from Firebase but using default values to avoid undefined errors. I've introduced a robust approach for arrays like programs, stories, and children.  It ensures either existing data or defaults are utilized. **Validating Home Rendering**

I'm now revisiting `pages/Home.tsx` to ensure graceful handling of potential loading states. Specifically, I'm checking if the `loading` state is active, and if so, to show a loading indicator instead of immediately rendering. The current logic initializes the `content` with defaults, but an issue might arise if `setContent` receives an empty object or potentially invalid data, so I am going to need to address that case. My analysis indicates the core issue is now resolved with the merging fix in `ContentContext.tsx`. I understand that ensuring default values are merged correctly, especially the `heroImage`, is critical. Defensive programming in `pages/Home.tsx` is secondary, although I will add a check. **Enhancing Offline Handling**

Any parsing errors are caught.  The goal is smooth operation even without an internet connection. I'll test offline scenarios thoroughly.

**Organization Name:** Mati Foundation
**Type:** Independent, community-driven humanitarian foundation
**Country:** Kenya
**Primary Region:** Coastal Kenya (Kilifi County context)
**Founder:** Matilda Kashindo

Mati Foundation is an independent, community-driven humanitarian foundation dedicated to supporting **vulnerable children and underserved communities**. The foundation was established through **direct grassroots involvement**, not corporate sponsorship, and focuses on **education, child welfare, health awareness, and sustainable community empowerment**.

The organization prioritizes **dignity-first support**, long-term transformation, transparency, and community-centered solutions.

---

## 2. FOUNDER PROFILE — MATILDA KASHINDO

**Founder:** Matilda Kashindo

Matilda Kashindo is a passionate humanitarian and community advocate whose close work with children and families inspired the creation of Mati Foundation. Her vision is to ensure that children not only survive, but **thrive through education, care, and opportunity**.

Her approach emphasizes:

* Respectful representation of children
* Grassroots, locally driven solutions
* Education as a foundation for sustainable change
* Integrity, accountability, and transparency

### Website-Ready Founder Narrative:

> Mati Foundation was founded by Matilda Kashindo out of a deep commitment to protecting children’s dignity and expanding access to education and care within underserved communities. Through years of grassroots involvement, she recognized that meaningful and lasting change begins with education, stable care, and community participation.

Tone must remain **calm, grounded, and respectful**, not promotional or dramatic.

---

## 3. MISSION, VISION & VALUES (AUTHORITATIVE)

### Mission

To uplift vulnerable children and communities by providing access to education, basic needs, and opportunities that promote dignity, resilience, and long-term transformation.

### Vision

A future where every child has access to education, care, and opportunity regardless of background or circumstance.

### Core Values

* Compassion and empathy
* Integrity and accountability
* Transparency in operations
* Community-centered solutions
* Sustainable long-term impact

---

## 4. WHO MATI FOUNDATION SERVES

* Orphaned and vulnerable children
* Low-income families
* Underserved communities across Kenya and Africa

Language must remain **dignity-focused** and never portray beneficiaries as helpless or dependent.

---

## 5. CORE PROGRAMS (USE THESE EXACT CATEGORIES)

### 1️⃣ Child Welfare & Care

Providing food, clothing, safe environments, and emotional support to vulnerable children while ensuring dignity and stability.

### 2️⃣ Education Support

Assisting with school fees, learning materials, uniforms, and educational continuity to help children access consistent learning opportunities.

### 3️⃣ Health & Wellbeing

Promoting child nutrition, health awareness, and basic wellbeing support as a foundation for growth and learning.

### 4️⃣ Community Empowerment

Strengthening families and communities through grassroots initiatives, local participation, and sustainable support systems.

Programs should be described as **long-term commitments**, not one-time aid.

---

## 6. SPONSORSHIP PROGRAM

### Sponsorship Philosophy

* One child, one future
* Respectful, transparent support
* Focus on education, care, and development

Sponsorship content must:

* Highlight education and wellbeing
* Avoid emotional pressure
* Present children as individuals with potential

---

## 7. GET INVOLVED

Support Mati Foundation through:

* **Bank donations** (local, transparent, Kenyan context — KSh)
* **Child sponsorship**
* **Item donations** (school supplies, clothing, food items)
* **Volunteering skills and time**
* **Partnerships**

Avoid flashy donation language. Keep it accessible and community-focused.

---

## 8. STORIES & UPDATES

Purpose:

* Share progress
* Highlight community impact
* Document milestones

Tone:

* Honest
* Hopeful
* Informative
* Non-sensational

---

## 9. GALLERY & MEDIA GUIDELINES (CONTENT RULE)

* Use authentic African imagery
* Children shown naturally and respectfully
* No exaggerated or staged scenes
* No exploitative visuals
* Mix of education, community life, and daily activities

Media exists to **build trust**, not shock or market.

---

## 10. BRAND TONE & PERSONALITY (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

Mati Foundation must feel:

* Calm
* Trustworthy
* Human
* Community-rooted
* Professional but warm

Avoid:

* Corporate NGO language
* Over-emotional appeals
* Loud or sales-driven copy

The brand voice should communicate:

> “Real people doing real work for real children.”

---

## 11. CONTENT IMPLEMENTATION INSTRUCTION

Apply this content consistently across:

* Home
* About
* Programs
* Sponsorship
* Get Involved
* Stories
* Gallery
* Contact
* Footer

Normalize wording so it feels written by **one organization with one voice**.

---

## 12. FINAL INSTRUCTION

This update is about **accuracy, trust, and clarity**.

Do not invent facts.
Do not exaggerate impact.
Do not change the organization’s values.

Update the website content now so it reflects **Mati Foundation as a credible, dignified, community-driven humanitarian organization**.

---

### END OF PROMPT

---

To implement video uploading across the platform, I will update the content types to support video media and enhance the admin panel and public pages to handle video playback.

payload.text: The image in the hero section is not clear

**Improving Image Clarity**

I've been examining the image in the hero section, trying to identify what's making its message unclear. Specifically, I'm now looking at `pages/ProgramDetail.tsx`. I believe the issue lies in how the image is presented; the solution is starting to clarify.


**Evaluating Image Placement**

I'm now comparing the image implementation in `ProgramDetail.tsx` and `Home.tsx`. The video approach in `ProgramDetail.tsx` seems interesting, but the scaling in `Home.tsx` could hold the key. I'm focusing on the effect of the opacity and gradient overlays in both. Analyzing these is revealing a path to improve image clarity.


**Analyzing Gradient Overlays**

I'm now diving into the gradient overlays in `Home.tsx`. I'm closely examining their impact on the `heroImage`. The dual gradient approach, particularly the opacity settings, seems promising, but I'm questioning whether they're too heavy-handed for image clarity. I'm considering lighter opacity values or different color combinations to ensure a good balance.


**Modifying Gradient Opacity**

I've been analyzing the opacity of the gradient overlays, especially the one on the homepage hero. The `from-teal-950/90` is far too dark and obscuring the image. I will reduce the opacity of the overlays to ensure the image is more visible while still providing contrast for the text. I'll also double-check the `ProgramDetail.tsx` gradient implementation, but the homepage is the primary focus.


**Reducing Overlay Opacity**

I'm now revising the gradient overlay in `Home.tsx`, noting the 90% opacity as the primary blocker to image clarity. I'll test lower values like 60% and 40%. Concurrently, I'm examining `ProgramDetail.tsx` to verify the video's opacity doesn't further obscure the image when the program has a video, as opposed to an image. The goal is a delicate balance of text readability and image visibility.


**Revising Opacity Values**

I'm now revising the gradient opacity for both the image and the video hero sections. For `Home.tsx`, I'll reduce the `from-teal-950/90` and `via-teal-900/70` values to enhance image visibility. Meanwhile, I'm removing the `opacity-80` from the video section in `ProgramDetail.tsx` to let the image shine more. The current opacity and gradient setup for both are obscuring the image, so modifications are needed to balance image visibility with text readability.


**Optimizing Hero Visibility**

I'm now implementing the latest adjustments. I've removed the `opacity-80` from the video section in `ProgramDetail.tsx`. I've also modified `Home.tsx` to reduce the hero overlay opacity, changing the gradient from `from-teal-950/90 via-teal-900/70` to `from-teal-950/80 via-teal-900/40 to-slate-900/10` to enhance visibility. I've also revised the top fade to `from-teal-950/80 via-teal-900/40 to-slate-900/10` to prevent image washout.


**Adjusting Opacity and Scaling**

I've significantly improved the image clarity by adjusting opacity and reviewing the hero image scaling. Removing the `opacity-80` from the video tag in `ProgramDetail.tsx` has helped greatly. Also, I've modified the gradient and opacity values in `Home.tsx`, and it's noticeably clearer. Moreover, I'm ensuring there aren't any unintended visual artifacts from `object-cover`.

I will adjust the **Home** page hero overlay to be more transparent so the background image is more visible and vivid. I will also ensure the **Program Detail** video/image is fully opaque (removing the darkening effect) so it appears clearly.
